steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Alvarez and colleagues claim that mapping time onto a front/back axis is 'not in fact ubiquitous.' The question asks which finding best supports this claim — meaning evidence that some group's temporal gestures are NOT anchored to a fixed front/back axis.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Yupno speakers typically use their left hand to make temporal gestures regardless of whether the gestures are past oriented or future oriented.: describes how Yupno speakers gesture in other contexts, not temporal gestures tied to a front/back axis. ✗
  • Choice B — When outdoors and referring to the future, Yupno speakers facing uphill gesture in front of themselves, whereas those facing downhill gesture behind themselves.: shows their temporal gestures shift with terrain orientation, meaning no fixed front/back axis governs them — directly supporting the claim that this mapping is not universal. ✓
  • Choice C — Yupno speakers were observed making temporal gestures both indoors and outdoors, though with greater frequency when indoors.: notes when temporal gestures occur, not whether they follow a consistent front/back axis. ✗
  • Choice D — Some Yupno grammatical structures used when talking about time are also used in Spanish.: addresses language structure, not gestural or spatial mapping of time. ✗
That is the correct answer.
